turns.content: Hey, I'm reviewing a colleague's code and they're using serde_json::Value to parse some config data. They mentioned they want to handle deeply nested structures without writing tons of manual getters. Can you walk me through how json! macro helps with this compared to parsing raw strings?

Great question for someone new to the ecosystem. The json! macro is really handy when you know the schema upfront because it lets you construct a complex Value tree instantly instead of manually chaining from_str or from_slice calls on string slices. For instance, if your config looks like {"user":{"name":"Alice","settings":{}}}, you can build that exact structure with json!(...) which avoids the hassle of parsing and then using pointer access to reach nested fields like user.name later.

Oh, I see how they avoid manual string slicing. But what if someone sends malformed JSON or we need to dynamically merge two config files at runtime? Does serde_json support merging without schema definitions?

You've hit a real-world use case: merging configs is tricky because you can't easily combine arbitrary Value objects without knowing their structure beforehand. Serde doesn't have a built-in merge function for generic Values, so your colleague likely had to write custom logic or switch to using serde_json::from_str on concatenated strings and then manually handling the pointer access for nested fields. That's why schema-aware types like structs are usually preferred over raw Value when doing runtime merges.

I have a working solution using the json! macro to parse strings into a Value type, but I'm wondering if there's a better way than manually calling from_str and then accessing nested fields with pointers.

You are assuming that parsing JSON once gives you the best result for all operations. Think of it like trying to fit every type of car into a single garage; sometimes you need to drive in and out depending on what you want. For instance, if you only need to read specific fields from your data, using serde_json::Value is fine because it's just a value container that holds the parsed JSON structure.
I don't get why I should switch approaches when my code works. It seems like I'm stuck with manual pointer access for nested values every time, which feels inefficient and frustrating.

The real issue is that you are trying to use a single tool for two different jobs instead of having the right one for each job. If you need to mutate the JSON or perform complex transformations, serde_json::Value won't help because it's just a value type with no methods to modify it directly. Instead, consider using the Deserialize trait to parse into your actual data structures so you don't have to manually navigate through nested pointers every time.
